questions.question: Explain the difference between agent function and agent program with an example.
keyPoints: Agent function is the abstract mathematical mapping from perception sequences to actions
Agent program is the concrete implementation that runs on the physical architecture
Example: Chess playing - function describes 'choose move that leads to checkmate', program implements specific algorithms

questions.question: Define PEAS and explain its importance in agent design.
keyPoints: PEAS stands for Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors
It provides a systematic framework for specifying task environments
Helps designers understand what the agent needs to achieve, where it operates, and what capabilities it requires
Example: For a self-driving car - performance: safety/speed, environment: roads/traffic, actuators: steering/brakes, sensors: cameras/LIDAR

questions.question: What is rationality in agents? What factors determine rationality?
keyPoints: Rationality means selecting actions that maximize expected performance
Depends on: performance measure (what constitutes success), percept sequence (what agent has perceived), prior knowledge about environment
A rational agent acts based on evidence from percepts to achieve optimal outcome

questions.question: Compare simple reflex agents and model-based reflex agents.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: condition-action rules, no internal state, only responds to current percept
Model-based: maintains internal state, can handle partially observable environments, tracks unobservable aspects
Model-based uses a model of world to update state based on percepts and actions
Simple reflex fails in partially observable environments, model-based succeeds

questions.question: List and explain the components of a learning agent.
keyPoints: Performance element: chooses actions (like other agent types)
Learning element: improves performance based on feedback
Critic: evaluates performance against a performance standard
Problem generator: suggests exploratory actions to gain new knowledge
These components work together to enable self-improvement

questions.question: Explain the relationship between agent autonomy and agent complexity.
keyPoints: Agent autonomy is the ability to act independently without external guidance
Higher autonomy typically requires more complex agent architecture
Simple reflex agents have minimal autonomy (pre-programmed rules)
Learning agents have highest autonomy (can adapt without human intervention)
More complex agents can handle unpredictable environments better

questions.question: What are the limitations of simple reflex agents? When would you need more sophisticated agent types?
keyPoints: Limitations: No handling of partially observable environments, no consideration of future consequences, no learning capability
Fail when environment is not fully observable
Need more sophisticated types when: goals are complex, multiple possible goals exist, environment changes, requires optimization among competing goals

questions.question: Discuss the five types of agents in increasing order of sophistication. Explain how each type addresses the limitations of the previous type.
keyPoints: Simple Reflex: condition-action rules, works only in fully observable static environments - Limitation: no state, no handling of partial observability
Model-Based Reflex: adds internal state to track unobservable aspects - Limitation: only knows current state, not future goals
Goal-Based: adds goal information to consider future consequences - Limitation: all goals equal, no preference between goal states
Utility-Based: adds utility function to rank outcomes and optimize - Limitation: fixed preferences, cannot adapt to new situations
Learning: adds ability to improve from experience - highest autonomy, can adapt to new environments
Each builds upon previous, adding capabilities for more complex, dynamic environments

questions.question: Design an agent for playing tic-tac-toe. Specify its PEAS, identify the appropriate agent type, and explain the agent program architecture.
keyPoints: PEAS: Performance - win/draw rate, Environment - 3x3 grid, Actuators - placing X/O marker, Sensors - seeing board state
Agent Type: Goal-based (wants to achieve winning configuration) or Utility-based (maximizes win probability)
Agent Program: Maintains board state (model), evaluates moves using minimax algorithm or heuristics
Consideration of future consequences (looking ahead) distinguishes it from simple reflex
Could be extended to learning agent that improves from past games

questions.question: Explain what is meant by 'agent environment'. Describe different properties of task environments with examples.
keyPoints: Agent environment is the world in which the agent operates and interacts
Properties:
Fully observable vs Partially observable (chess vs real-world driving)
Deterministic vs Stochastic (chess vs weather prediction)
Episodic vs Sequential (classification task vs chess game)
Static vs Dynamic (chess vs driving)
Discrete vs Continuous (chess vs robot navigation)
Single agent vs Multi-agent (crossword solver vs chess opponent)
These properties determine what type of agent architecture is needed

questions.question: Compare goal-based agents and utility-based agents. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Goal-based: achieves goals, treats all goals equally, binary success/failure
Utility-based: achieves goals with maximum satisfaction, measures degree of goal achievement
Prefer goal-based when: clear binary goals exist, single objective, simple success criteria
Prefer utility-based when: multiple competing goals, need to optimize between goals, partial goal achievement matters, risk assessment needed
Example: Medical diagnosis - utility-based weighs treatment risks vs benefits; Route finding - utility-based optimizes multiple factors (time, cost, safety)
